CSCE 633 Syllabus

Section 600 (33086)

Machine Learning

Fall 2025 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 09:35AM

End Time: 10:50AM

Start Date: 08/25/2025

End Date: 12/16/2025

Meeting Location:  EABB 106

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Tianbao Yang

Email:  tianbao-yang@tamu.edu

Office:  PETR 436

Phone:  9798458873

Office Hours

Tuesday: 11am - 12pm

Course Description

Machine learning is a field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without

being explicitly programmed. It is the core of AI. In the past decade, machine

learning has created tremendous value with its application in various domains,

including information retrieval, speech recognition, computer vision, understanding

of human genome, natural language processing, etc. In this class, students will not

only learn the fundamental machine learning techniques, but also gain the hands-on

experience by applying these techniques and developing new techniques for solving

problems from the real world. In addition, students will learn some recent advances in

deep learning and AI. Topics of interest include: (1) Regression (least square

regression, lasso). (2) Classification (support vector machines, logistic regression).

(3) Kernel Methods. (4) Unsupervised methods; (5) Self-supervised learning; (6)

Stochastic Optimization. (7) Deep Learning. By the end of class, students are

expected to accomplish a project related to machine learning in a group.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 420 or CSCE 625.

You should be comfortable with some Math and Programming.

Math Background: Linear algebra, Calculus, Probability and Statistics

Programming Background: Python, Pytorch

Preferably access to GPUs (e.g.,  Google Colab ).

Special Course Designation

None

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Have a good understanding of basic machine learning methods.

Know how to train a ML model

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Late submission caused by excused absence (defined in  Student rule 7 ) will be

accepted. Excused absence will not be counted towards late days. Contact the

instructor if you think you have an extremely rare circumstance for which we should

make an exception. This policy is to ensure that feedback can be given in a timely

manner.

Unexpected excused absences: In cases where prior notification of excused absence is

not feasible (e.g., accident or emergency) the student must provide notification by the

end of the second working day after the absence, including an explanation of why

notice could not be sent prior to the class.

Each homework assignment due by midnight (11:59pm) on the due date. Late

homeworks will not be graded so please just hand in whatever you have completed.

Late project submissions will not be accepted.

Additional Course Information

Collaboration

Both homework assignments and projects are team-based, and each student must join

a team of 2–3 members to collaborate. Only one copy of the homework or project

needs to be submitted per team, and every submission must clearly acknowledge each

member’s contributions. If a student is found to be uncooperative, they may be

removed from the team and required to form or join a new team if necessary.

AI Statement

The use of AI tools such as large language models for completing homework

assignments, exams, or the course project is strictly prohibited.  However, it is

acceptable to use them for tasks such as editing your writing or assisting in debugging

your code.
